mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects a strengthening labor market, which drives higher aggregate demand and corporate earnings, leading to positive equity returns. This is a persistent, always-on signal of economic health.
evidence_refs: fact:Post-2009 recovery has been heavily focused on labor market improvement.
failure_condition: A positive 3-month change in PAYEMS is followed by negative median 20-day S&P 500 returns over a rolling 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: A rapid increase in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals a cyclical downturn and rising recession risk. This shock to household income and confidence leads to equity market declines as future earnings expectations are revised downwards.
failure_condition: Instances where the trigger condition is met are not followed by negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 days in at least 50% of cases during a walk-forward test.

mechanism_narrative: When inflation (CPIAUCSL) accelerates while the central bank fails to tighten policy (FEDFUNDS remains flat or falls), real rates decline and stagflationary risks mount. This policy error erodes corporate profit margins and investor confidence, causing negative equity returns.
failure_condition: The interaction signal (high CPIAUCSL momentum AND low FEDFUNDS momentum) fails to generate a rank information coefficient below -0.03 over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-recessionary environment, accelerating industrial production (INDPRO) confirms a durable recovery in the manufacturing and supply side of the economy. This leads to upward revisions in earnings forecasts and drives positive equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Current context is recovery from 2008 GFC.
failure_condition: The signal's positive correlation with forward returns is statistically insignificant (p > 0.10) during periods identified as economic recovery phases.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp, negative month-over-month surprise in retail sales (RSAFS) indicates an abrupt slowdown in consumer spending, which constitutes a large portion of GDP. This leads to immediate downward revisions of revenue forecasts for consumer-facing companies, negatively impacting the S&P 500.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending is ~70% of US GDP.
failure_condition: Trigger events are not followed by a negative mean 20-day forward return with statistical significance (t-test p-value > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) exceeds a high threshold, it indicates restrictive monetary policy that increases the cost of capital for firms and makes risk-free assets more attractive than equities. This higher discount rate reduces the present value of future earnings, causing stock prices to fall.
evidence_refs: fact:Historical market performance during Fed tightening cycles (e.g., 1994, 2000, 2006).
failure_condition: In periods where FEDFUNDS is above 4.0%, the average 60-day forward return on the S&P 500 is not statistically different from periods where it is below 4.0%.

mechanism_narrative: An unexpected increase in housing starts (HOUST) signals strong consumer confidence and forward-looking demand for durable goods and related services. This positive signal for a key cyclical sector drives short-term positive sentiment for the broader market.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing is a key leading indicator of economic activity.
failure_condition: The rank correlation between month-over-month changes in HOUST and 5-day forward S&P 500 returns is not positive and significant over the full sample.

mechanism_narrative: The combination of slowing real GDP growth (GDPC1) and a rising unemployment rate (UNRATE) is a classic recessionary signal. The interaction of weakening aggregate demand and a deteriorating labor market confirms a broad-based economic slowdown, leading to significant equity market declines.
failure_condition: Periods where GDPC1 q/q growth is negative and UNRATE m/m change is positive are not followed by negative 60-day S&P 500 returns more than 60% of the time.
